author.name: Abinash Sahoo
author.headline: Full Stack Developer ASP .NET | C# • React • ASP.NET Core • Node• SQL• docker | Performance Optimization, Web APIs, System Design | 2+ Years Experience (specialized in Backend)

post.text:
⚠️ Your Express route is freezing every user's request — and you might not even know it.

Node.js runs on a single thread. One heavy synchronous operation in a route = the entire event loop blocked for ALL concurrent users.

Here's how to fix it:

🔵 Worker Threads
→ Offload CPU-heavy tasks (parsing, encryption, ML inference)
→ Runs parallel to the main thread, shared memory
→ Use a thread pool for high-traffic routes

🔵 Child Processes
→ For isolated or legacy work (Python scripts, shell commands)
→ Full process isolation — crashes won't affect your server

🔵 setImmediate Chunking
→ Break large array processing into smaller chunks
→ Yields back to the event loop between each chunk
→ No extra dependencies needed

🔵 Job Queues (Bull / BullMQ)
→ For anything that takes more than 1–2 seconds
→ Respond instantly with a jobId, process in the background
→ Built-in retries, priority, scheduling

🔵 Streaming
→ For large datasets — never buffer everything in memory
→ Pipe DB/file streams directly to the response

💡 The rule that saves you:
If it takes more than a few milliseconds of CPU time → get it OFF the main thread.

And remember:
✅ I/O work (DB queries, file reads) = just use async/await, Node handles it natively
❌ CPU work (computation, transforms) = needs a worker or queue

Which of these have you used in production? 👇

#NodeJS #ExpressJS #BackendDevelopment #JavaScript #SoftwareEngineering #WebDevelopment

🚀 Excited to share one of my most ambitious projects University ERP, a full-stack Enterprise Resource Planning system I built during my 3rd semester!

Managing a university involves hundreds of moving parts students, faculty, attendance, exams, fees, and more. I wanted to build something that brings all of it under one roof, and that's exactly what University-ERP does.

🏗️ What I Built

A complete, role-based University Management System with dedicated portals for Students, Faculty, and Admins each with their own dashboards, permissions, and workflows.

🔧 Tech Stack

▸ Frontend: React 19 + React Router v7 + Material UI (MUI) v7
▸ Backend: Node.js + Express.js (RESTful API)
▸ Database: MySQL with mysql2 driver
▸ Auth & Security: JWT (JSON Web Tokens) + Bcrypt password hashing
▸ File Handling: Multer (profile pictures, document uploads)
▸ PDF Generation: PDFKit (reports, receipts, transcripts)
▸ Email Notifications: Nodemailer (automated alerts & confirmations)

📦 Core Modules

👨‍🎓 Student Management
▸ Student registration, profile management, and enrollment tracking
▸ Role-based access so students only see what's relevant to them

👩‍🏫 Faculty & Staff Management
▸ Faculty profiles, course assignments, and department-level organization
▸ Admin controls for onboarding and managing staff records

📋 Attendance Tracking
▸ Faculty can mark and manage attendance per class/session
▸ Students can view their attendance records in real-time
▸ Automated alerts via Nodemailer when attendance drops below threshold

📝 Exam & Results Management
▸ Admins and faculty can schedule exams and upload results
▸ Students can view their grades and download PDF report cards generated via PDFKit
▸ Full results history maintained in the database

🔐 Security & Architecture Highlights

✅ JWT-based stateless authentication with role-based route protection
✅ Bcrypt-hashed passwords — no plaintext storage
✅ Multer-powered file uploads with validation
✅ Clean RESTful API design separating concerns between frontend and backend
✅ Modular Express.js routing for scalability

💡 Key Learnings

Building this in 3rd semester was a serious challenge and an incredible learning experience. I got hands-on with:
▸ Full-stack architecture and API design
▸ Relational database design & query optimization with MySQL
▸ JWT auth flows and secure password handling
▸ PDF generation and automated email pipelines
▸ Building complex, multi-role UIs with React and MUI

🔗 Check out the full project here:
👉 https://lnkd.in/g2vDyhwV

author.name: Mohd Fardeen
author.headline: Full Stack Web Developer BCA @ DSEU’26 | Building Real Projects

post.text:
Most people think Node.js is a programming language.
I did too when I first started learning backend.

But that’s not actually true.

🧠 Day 12/21 – Web Development Execution Challenge

Node.js is NOT a language.

It’s a runtime environment.

When I first heard “Node.js backend,” I assumed it meant a new language for servers. But the reality is simpler and more interesting.

JavaScript is the language.

Node.js is what allows JavaScript to run outside the browser.

That’s the key idea.

Instead of running inside Chrome or Firefox, your JavaScript code can now run directly on your machine or server.

And that changes everything.

Under the hood, Node.js uses  Google’s V8 engine — the same engine that runs JavaScript in Chrome.

But Node adds extra capabilities on top of it.

For example, Node provides APIs that allow JavaScript to:

• Work with the file system 
• Handle HTTP requests 
• Create servers 
• Manage processes 
• Work with streams and networking 

Which means JavaScript is no longer limited to browser interactions.

Another interesting part of Node.js is its architecture.

Instead of creating a new thread for every request, Node uses an event-driven, non-blocking architecture powered by the event loop.

What that means in simple terms:

The server doesn’t stop and wait for one task to finish.

It keeps handling other tasks while waiting for operations like database queries or file reads.

Because of that, Node is:

• Lightweight 
• Fast for I/O-heavy applications 
• Great for APIs and real-time systems 

That’s why Node is commonly used for:

• REST APIs 
• Real-time applications (like chats) 
• Streaming services 
• Microservices 

But something else clicked for me today.

Node.js is not “just backend.”

With Node you can also:

• Build backend APIs (Express, Fastify) 
• Create real-time apps (Socket.io) 
• Build CLI tools 
• Automate workflows 
• Run build tools like Webpack, Vite, or ESLint 
• Power frameworks like Next.js 
• Even build desktop apps using Electron 

In fact, whenever you run:

npm install 
npm run dev 
npm run build 

You are already using Node.js.

Understanding this changes how you see the JavaScript ecosystem.

It’s not “frontend vs backend.”

It’s one language.

Running in different environments.

Day 12/21 ✅

Today wasn’t about building a project.

It was about understanding the tool that powers almost the entire modern JavaScript ecosystem.

And that perspective shift matters.

Learning from experts Sheryians Coding School 

#NodeJS #JavaScript #BackendDevelopment #BuildInPublic #21DaysChallenge #SoftwareEngineering

post.text:
🚀 Serving Static Files in Express.js – A Fundamental Backend Skill
While building web applications with Node.js and Express.js, one important concept every backend developer should understand is serving static files.
Static files such as HTML, CSS, JavaScript, images, and fonts are essential for delivering the frontend of a web application. With Express, this process becomes simple and efficient using the built-in middleware:
app.use(express.static("public"));
This single line allows the server to automatically serve files from a specific directory, making assets easily accessible to users.
Understanding how static files work helps developers:
 ✔ Improve application structure
 ✔ Deliver frontend assets efficiently
 ✔ Build scalable full-stack applications
Mastering these small but important concepts is what gradually turns a developer into a strong backend engineer.

post.text:
🚀 Express.js Revision – Day 1
Today I started revising Express.js, one of the most widely used backend frameworks for Node.js. Instead of jumping directly into complex APIs, I focused on revisiting the core fundamentals of how Express works.
🔹 What is Express?
Express is a fast, minimalist, and unopinionated web framework for Node.js that helps us build servers and web applications efficiently.
When a user sends a request from the browser:
Browser → Server → Express → Response back to the client
Express acts as a layer that helps Node.js handle requests and responses easily.
🔹 Library vs Framework (Important Concept)
Library
A collection of prewritten code.
The developer controls when and how to use it.
Used for small functionality changes.
Framework
Provides a structure for the entire application.
Control flow is inverted — the framework decides when our code runs.
We plug our logic into the framework.
Express is a framework, meaning it provides the structure for building web servers.
🔹 Steps to Start an Express Server
1️⃣ Initialize project
npm init -y
2️⃣ Install Express
npm install express
3️⃣ Import Express in JavaScript
const express = require("express");
4️⃣ Create an Express application
const app = express();
5️⃣ Start the server
app.listen(3000, () => {
 console.log("Server running on port 3000");
});
At this stage, the server starts and waits for incoming requests on localhost:3000.
🔹 Handling Requests in Express
Express provides middleware functions such as app.use() which run whenever a request is received.
app.use((req, res) => {
 res.send("Hello World");
});
Here:
req (Request Object) → Represents the incoming HTTP request
res (Response Object) → Used to send a response back to the client
Important thing to understand:
HTTP requests arrive in text format, but Express parses them into JavaScript objects (req and res) so developers can easily work with them.
🔹 Sending Responses
Express provides the res.send() method to send data back to the browser.
It can send:
• Strings
• Objects
• Arrays
• Buffers
Example:
res.send({ color: "red" });
Even though this is a JavaScript object, the browser receives it as JSON.
📌 Key Concepts Revised Today
✔ Express framework fundamentals
✔ Difference between library and framework
✔ Creating an Express server
✔ Understanding req and res objects
✔ Using app.use() middleware
✔ Sending responses using res.send()
Consistency is the goal.
Starting a daily backend revision series to strengthen fundamentals.
#ExpressJS #NodeJS #BackendDevelopment #WebDevelopment #JavaScript #LearningInPublic

post.text:
Built a fun little weekend project — and learned more than I expected. 🛩️

Introducing PaperPlane — an anonymous messaging app where anyone can send you a message, no account needed. Think NGL, but with its own personality.

I built this by following Hitesh Choudhary's Next.js tutorial — but I didn't stop there. I used it as a foundation to actually understand the stack, then extended it with features and UX decisions that were entirely my own:

→ OTP email verification for receivers
→ AI-suggested messages based on a receiver's personality (a bit of prompt engineering with a free LLM via OpenRouter)
→ A day-to-night theme progression across the full user journey
→ Edge cases that most tutorial projects never think about — wrong redirects, username conflicts, graceful 404s
→ Rate limiting on sensitive API routes (post-build addition!)

Stack: Next.js (fullstack) · NextAuth.js · MongoDB · shadcn/ui + Tailwind · Nodemailer · OpenRouter

The real win wasn't the app itself — it was the moment copy-pasted code stopped working and I had to actually understand what was happening to fix it. That's where the learning lives.

Not a project that'll cure cancer. But a genuinely fun one that levelled up how I think about fullstack development. 🚀

🔗 Live demo in the comments — would love for you to send me a paperplane 👀

post.text:
I Started Learning Node.js to Strengthen My Backend Skills
Instead of jumping straight into frameworks, I focused on understanding how Node.js actually works.
Here’s what I’ve covered so far:
Core Foundations
• How Node.js executes JavaScript outside the browser
 • Why there is no window or document object on the server
 • The difference between browser runtime and server runtime
 • Event-driven, non-blocking architecture fundamentals
Modules & Code Organization
• CommonJS module system
 • module.exports and require()
 • Named exports vs aggregated exports
 • Structuring code across multiple files
Understanding this properly makes scaling projects easier later.
Core Built-in Modules
• fs → Reading and writing files
 • path → Handling cross-platform file paths
 • os → Accessing system-level information
Instead of just using frameworks blindly, I wanted clarity on how backend logic interacts with the operating system.
Why This Matters
Many developers jump directly into Express or NestJS without understanding:
How Node handles I/O
How modules are resolved
How the event loop behaves
How blocking operations affect performance
I’m focusing on fundamentals first.
Because frameworks change.
Core runtime understanding does not.
Now here’s the uncomfortable question you should ask yourself next:
Have you studied:
The event loop phases?
Microtasks vs macrotasks?
Streams?
Buffers?
Async vs sync FS trade-offs?
How Node handles concurrency internally?
If not, you’re still at beginner level — and that’s fine.
Just don’t confuse starting with mastering.

author.name: Sachin Verma
author.headline: Aspiring Full-Stack Developer | Java • Python • HTML/CSS/JS | B.Tech IT Student | Passionate Coder

post.text:
🚀 Day 78 Building My First Backend Server with Express.js

Today I built my first basic backend server using Node.js and Express.js — understanding how servers actually start, listen, and respond to requests.
This was the foundation of real backend development 💻🔥

🔹 Project Setup
• Created a new project folder
• Initialized Node.js project using npm init
• Installed Express using npm install express
• Created index.js as the entry point

🔹 Understanding the Core Concepts
📦 require() Function
Used to import modules like Express into our Node.js application.

🧰 Express
A backend framework that provides tools to build web servers easily.

🚪 What is a Port?
Ports are like “doors” of a machine.
If your app listens on port 3000, requests must enter through that specific door.

🔹 Starting the Server

const express = require('express');
const app = express();
const PORT = 3000;

app.listen(PORT, () => {
 console.log('Server started successfully');
});

This binds the application to port 3000 and starts listening for requests.

🔹 Creating a Basic Route

app.get('/', (req, res) => {
 res.send('Hello World');
});

• Defined a GET route
• Understood request–response lifecycle
• Tested on http://localhost:3000
• Successfully ran my first backend server ✅

🔹 Important Learnings
• Backend apps must listen on a specific port
• .listen() binds the server to that port
• Routes define how the server responds
• GET, POST, PUT, DELETE are core HTTP methods (deep dive coming next)

📌 Key Takeaway
Today wasn’t about complex logic —
• It was about understanding how a backend server is created, started, and how it handles requests.
• Strong foundations lead to scalable systems 🚀
• Next: Exploring HTTP methods in detail & API testing 💪

#BackendDevelopment #NodeJS #ExpressJS #WebDevelopment #LearningInPublic #FullStackJourney

author.name: Mohammad Aman
author.headline: Full-Stack Developer | Leveling Up Skills and Impact, Every Day

post.text:
Most developers say they “know Node.js”.

But many don’t fully understand how it actually works under the hood.
Node.js is not just “JavaScript running on the server”.
It’s a layered system built from several components working together:
• JavaScript (ECMAScript)
• V8 JavaScript Engine
• Node.js Runtime APIs
• libuv Event Loop & Thread Pool
• Operating System Kernel

Understanding this architecture explains:
• Why Node.js can handle thousands of concurrent connections
• How the event loop actually works
• Why some operations block while others don’t
• When you should use Worker Threads

I wrote a deep dive explaining the full architecture step-by-step.
If you're a JavaScript, Backend, or Full-Stack developer, this will give you a much clearer mental model of how Node.js works internally.
Read the article here:
https://lnkd.in/gENx3vMn

post.text:
🧵 Worker Threads vs Clusters in Node.js — do you know when to use which?

Most developers know Node.js is single-threaded. But when your app needs real parallelism, you have two powerful tools at your disposal. Here's the breakdown:

⚙️ Worker Threads
→ Same process, shared memory
→ Perfect for CPU-heavy tasks: image processing, ML inference, file parsing
→ Communicate via postMessage() or SharedArrayBuffer
→ Lightweight — low overhead to spin up

🖥️ Clusters
→ Multiple processes, isolated memory
→ Perfect for scaling HTTP servers across all CPU cores
→ Communicate via IPC (inter-process communication)
→ Built on child_process.fork() under the hood
→ A single worker crash won't take down the whole app

🔑 The key mental model:
• CPU-bound work? → Worker Threads
• Network/I/O scaling? → Clusters

And here's the pro move: combine both. Use Clusters to scale across cores, and Worker Threads within each process for heavy computation.

Drop your Node.js parallelism questions below 👇

#NodeJS #JavaScript #BackendDevelopment #SoftwareEngineering #WebDevelopment

author.headline: Full Stack AI Developer | AI-ML | JavaScript Developer | Gen-AI | @codeXnam

post.text:
Backend Revision Series — Post 2/15
Topic: What is Node.js?
When I first started learning backend development, I kept hearing about Node.js everywhere.
At first, I thought Node.js was a framework like Express.
But it’s actually something different.
👉 Node.js is a runtime environment that allows JavaScript to run outside the browser.
Normally, JavaScript runs inside browsers like Chrome.
 But Node.js lets us run JavaScript on the server to build backend applications.
That means with JavaScript we can now build:
• APIs
 • Servers
 • Real-time apps
 • Full-stack applications
One reason Node.js is so fast is because it uses the V8 JavaScript engine (the same engine used by Google Chrome).
Another important concept is its non-blocking, event-driven architecture, which allows Node.js to handle many requests efficiently.
That’s why many modern applications use Node.js for backend development.
Learning this made me realize something interesting:
👉 JavaScript is no longer just a frontend language.
You can build the entire application with it.
Tomorrow I’ll cover:
 How Node.js actually works (Event Loop Explained).
#NodeJS #BackendDevelopment #JavaScript #WebDevelopment #LearningInPublic

author.headline: Experienced Laravel developer and Core Java programmer with a passion for building scalable solutions

post.text:
🚀 System Design: How to Send Millions of Emails
Sending millions of emails from an application is not as simple as using a loop.
If done incorrectly, it can crash the server or get the email service blocked.
Here is a common scalable approach:
1️⃣ Queue System
Instead of sending emails directly, push email jobs into a queue.
2️⃣ Workers
Multiple workers process the queue and send emails in the background.
3️⃣ Batch Processing
Emails are processed in batches instead of sending all at once.
4️⃣ Rate Limiting
Control how many emails are sent per second to avoid provider limits.
5️⃣ Retry Mechanism
Failed emails are retried automatically.
6️⃣ Monitoring & Logging
Track delivery status and failures.
💡 This architecture helps handle millions of emails without slowing down the main application.
Technologies commonly used:
Laravel Queues • Redis • RabbitMQ • Background Workers
#SystemDesign #BackendDevelopment #Laravel #SoftwareEngineering

post.text:
Important JavaScript Concepts Every Developer Must Master

JavaScript is not just about syntax or frameworks  it’s about understanding how the language behaves at runtime. These notes focus on the most important JavaScript concepts that directly impact real-world applications, performance, and interview outcomes.

Instead of surface-level explanations, this collection breaks down execution flow, memory behavior, and async handling, helping developers move from trial-and-error coding to predictable, confident development.

These concepts form the foundation for frameworks like React, Angular, and Node.js, and mastering them makes learning any new library significantly easier.

Key Concepts Covered

Core JavaScript Fundamentals

Execution Context & Call Stack

Scope, Lexical Environment & Scope Chain

Hoisting (var, let, const)

Value vs Reference

Functions & Objects

this keyword (implicit, explicit, arrow)

Closures & memory behavior

Higher-Order Functions

Prototypes & Inheritance

Asynchronous JavaScript

Callbacks & callback hell

Promises & microtask queue

Async/Await execution flow

Event Loop (microtasks vs macrotasks)

Advanced & Interview-Critical Topics

Debouncing & Throttling

Currying & Function Composition

Shallow vs Deep Copy

Equality (== vs ===)

Polyfills & custom implementations

Performance & Best Practices

Memory leaks & garbage collection basics

Immutability & state updates

Optimizing loops & async operations

Writing predictable, clean JS

Why These Concepts Matter

Frequently asked in frontend & full-stack interviews

Essential for writing efficient React code

Help debug complex async bugs faster

Build strong fundamentals for system design

Who Should Learn This

Frontend developers

Full-stack engineers

React / Angular developers

Anyone preparing for JavaScript interviews

post.text:
ARE YOU BUILDING OR BURNING BRIDGES?>Ghosting isn’t just a dating term anymore. It’s become part of the hiring process—and it goes both ways.
One thing that still surprises me after decades in recruiting is how often someone can be enthusiastic about an opportunity… or excited for you to work on a search… and then suddenly disappear. No returned calls. No emails. Just silence.
Why does this happen?
Sometimes priorities change. Sometimes another opportunity appears. Sometimes people simply avoid an uncomfortable conversation.
But here’s the reality: every interaction in business builds—or burns—a bridge.
In recruiting, relationships come full circle. The candidate you ignore today might be the hiring manager tomorrow. The recruiter you ghost today might be the one representing your dream opportunity five years from now.
A quick call or a short email saying “circumstances changed” takes two minutes.
But the professionalism it shows lasts much longer.
The hiring world is smaller than people think.
Close the loop.
Protect your reputation.
Don’t burn bridges you may need to cross again.

author.name: Amit Dewangan
author.headline: Aspiring SDE

post.text:
I used Node.js for a long time…
but today I finally explored what happens behind the scenes. ⚙️
Node.js isn’t just JavaScript running on a server.
It’s powered by a few powerful components:
• V8 Engine → Converts JavaScript into machine code
• Event Loop → Handles asynchronous operations efficiently
• libuv → Enables non-blocking I/O and manages the thread pool
• Event Queue → Stores incoming requests
• Thread Pool → Executes heavy tasks in the background
All these pieces work together to make Node.js fast, scalable, and non-blocking.
Hitesh Choudhary | Piyush Garg | Akash Kadlag | Jay Kadlag 
Understanding the internal architecture makes you appreciate why Node can handle thousands of concurrent requests with ease.
What backend concept are you exploring this week? 🚀
#NodeJS #BackendDevelopment #JavaScript #WebDevelopment #EventLoop #SoftwareEngineering #CodingJourney #chaicode

Building My Own Blog API(Part 5)

🚀 Built a RESTful Blog API using Node.js and Express!

In this project, I developed a simple Blog Post Management API that allows users to create and retrieve posts using Node.js and Express.js.

post.text:
🚀 Strengthening Backend Fundamentals with Node.js & Express

Today I spent time revisiting and practicing some core backend development concepts while building a small Notes API using Node.js and Express.

Instead of only reading theory, I focused on implementing the concepts step by step.

Here’s what I worked on today:

🔹 Setting up a Node.js project using npm
🔹 Understanding the role of package.json and dependency management
🔹 Creating an Express server and starting it using `app.listen()`
🔹 Understanding request (`req`) and response (`res`) objects
🔹 Using **middleware with `express.json()` to handle JSON request bodies
🔹 Building REST API endpoints
🔹 Testing APIs using Postman

Implemented endpoints:

✔ POST /notes→ Create a note
✔ GET /notes→ Retrieve all notes

This small exercise helped reinforce how REST APIs work and how backend servers process requests and send structured JSON responses.

Next steps in my backend learning journey:

➡ Implement PATCH and DELETE endpoints.
➡ Learn proper backend project structure (controllers, routes, services)
➡ Connect APIs with a database.

Learning step by step and documenting the journey. 💻

post.text:
🚀 Excited to share my latest MERN Stack project — Sakhi Suraksha!💜

On the occasion of Women's Day, I built Sakhi Suraksha, a women safety platform designed to provide quick access to emergency help, community support, and safety resources.
The goal of this project is to leverage technology to create a safer and more supportive digital environment for women.

🔹 Key Features
🚨 SOS Emergency Alert
Users can trigger an SOS alert that automatically sends their live location via email to emergency contacts for immediate assistance.
🤝 Community Support
A space where users can share experiences, ask for help, and support each other. Community posts are stored securely using MongoDB.
📍 Nearby Safe Places
Quickly locate important places such as police stations, hospitals, and helpline centers, with direct access to Google Maps for navigation.
💡 Safety Tips
Helpful safety guidelines to increase awareness and help users stay prepared in difficult situations.
✨ Modern & Interactive UI
Built with a clean and responsive design using Tailwind CSS, featuring gradient themes, glassmorphism cards, and smooth animations for a better user experience.

🔹 Tech Stack
Frontend: React.js, Tailwind CSS
Backend: Node.js, Express.js
Database: MongoDB
API Communication: Axios
Email Alerts: Nodemailer

This project helped me strengthen my skills in full-stack development, API integration, and building meaningful applications that solve real-world problems.

author.name: Blue Heron Media, LLC
author.headline: 2 followers

post.text:
Let’s say you want a form on your website where a potential client fills it out and you immediately get an email to your inbox. Most form builders let you create a form just fine, but the admin notification (the email set up where you get an email with all that form information) might not go to your inbox so easily. 

Most email clients like Gmail and Outlook will flag an email that’s sent from a random website. You need a tool that will authenticate your emails so they look legitimate when delivering to most email clients. If you don’t consider yourself “techy,” you may get lost in the details of how to set all this up on the back end of your website.

I’ve talked to many people that say they created their website using an AI tool, but have no idea how to make any changes going forward. You need a human to help you when this happens. But why not hire the human from the beginning? 

Let’s avoid the dystopian novels and movies. Save humanity by hiring one.
If you need a custom website built, talk to me.

post.text:
🚀 Excited to share something I’ve been building.
I’ve launched AI Interviewer — an end-to-end AI-powered interview platform designed to help recruiters screen candidates faster, more consistently, and with real data.
Hiring is often slow, manual, and inconsistent. The goal of this platform is simple: make candidate screening smarter, structured, and scalable with AI.
What the platform does
• Conducts structured AI-driven interviews
• Records candidate video interviews using WebRTC
• Stores resumes and interview videos securely on AWS S3
• Generates AI-based interview feedback and scoring
• Supports recruiter workflows, admin controls, and role-based access
• Built with a multi-tenant B2B foundation for organizations and hiring teams
Tech stack behind the product
Frontend: Next.js 14, React 18, TypeScript
Backend: Spring Boot 3.2.5, Java 17
Data & infrastructure: PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka, Docker
AI layer: Ollama (self-hosted) with optional OpenAI fallback
Deployment: Vercel (frontend) + AWS EC2 (backend)
CI/CD: GitHub Actions
Engineering focus
• Production-ready architecture
• Event-driven async evaluation pipeline
• Secure authentication with JWT + RBAC
• Monitoring, health checks, migrations, and automated deployments
• Built around real interview workflows and recruiter needs
Current status
The core platform is fully implemented and deployable.
Interview lifecycle, feedback generation, resume/video handling, admin operations, and B2B organization features are all in place.
Now I’m looking for early users and feedback.
If you are:
• A recruiter or hiring team wanting faster screening
• A founder building a team
• An HR leader exploring AI-driven hiring
I’d love for you to try it and share feedback.
👉 Early access:
https://lnkd.in/gQFCf3HM
Comment “INTERVIEW AI” or DM me if you'd like access.
#AI #Hiring #Recruitment #HRTech #SaaS #SpringBoot #Nextjs #Java #AWS #Startup #ProductBuild #InterviewPlatform

post.text:
Location: PWD, Islamabad
Company: O'Cyber
Salary: Up to 150,000 PKR/month
Experience: 1–2 Years
Qualification: BSCS / BSSE / BSIT (or equivalent)

About O'Cyber:
O'Cyber is a proficient Software Solutions Company based in Islamabad, Pakistan, offering services in Web Development, Mobile App Development, Cloud Architecture Management, UI/UX Design, and Automation Services. We are focused on delivering high-quality solutions that help businesses reach their target audiences, retain customers, and grow sustainably.

Role Description:
We are looking for a motivated Backend Developer to join our growing team in Islamabad. This is a full-time, on-site role where you’ll build scalable backend systems, contribute to microservices-based architecture, and work closely with cross-functional teams to deliver high-performance products. You’ll primarily work with Golang and Python, building APIs and real-time services using modern backend practices.

Key Responsibilities:
Backend Development: Design, develop, and maintain backend services using Golang and Python
Microservices: Build and maintain microservices-based systems with clean service boundaries and scalable architecture
API Development: Develop and integrate REST APIs and GraphQL endpoints for client-server communication
Real-time & Communication: Implement services using WebSockets for real-time features and gRPC for efficient service-to-service communication
Database Management: Work with relational and NoSQL databases including Redis, MongoDB, PostgreSQL, MySQL
Cloud & Deployment: Assist in deployments and infrastructure tasks using AWS, Docker, and Serverless solutions
Version Control: Collaborate using Git and GitHub, following best practices for branching, code reviews, and CI workflows
Debugging & Optimization: Troubleshoot performance bottlenecks and improve system reliability and scalability

Qualifications:
Bachelor’s degree in Computer Science (BSCS), Software Engineering (BSSE), Information Technology (BSIT), or equivalent
1–2 years of backend development experience
Hands-on experience with Golang and Python
Good understanding of microservices architecture, APIs, and backend fundamentals
Familiarity with REST, GraphQL, WebSockets, and gRPC
Experience working with databases: Redis, MongoDB, PostgreSQL, MySQL
Strong grasp of writing clean, maintainable, and modular code
Comfortable working in a collaborative environment and communicating effectively

Required Skills:
Golang (must-have)
Python (must-have)
Microservices (must-have)
REST APIs / GraphQL (must-have)
WebSockets & gRPC (must-have)
Databases: Redis, MongoDB, PostgreSQL, MySQL (must-have)
Git & GitHub (must-have)
AWS, Docker, Serverless (must-have / strongly preferred)

Plus (Optional / Nice-to-Have):
Experience with Node.js
Knowledge of C++

#hiring #backend #developer #go #golang #job #jobpost

author.name: CareerXperts Consulting
author.headline: 928,241 followers

post.text:
The numbers in India’s .NET ecosystem look impressive at first glance. Profiles are abundant, experience seems deep, and the talent pool appears massive. 

Yet many founders building serious product companies keep running into the same obstacle: senior engineering roles that remain open far longer than expected.

The explanation is not obvious, and it rarely shows up in hiring dashboards or LinkedIn searches. Something deeper is shaping the distribution of engineering capability in the market.

In this newsletter, we take a closer look at what’s really happening inside the .NET talent landscape and why understanding it could change how you approach hiring entirely.

#DotNet #SoftwareEngineering #Innovation #Technology #ProductEngineering #Leadership #Hiring #Startups #CSharp #Java

This is a complex, multi-phase product built on React / Next.js, React Native, Node.js, and PostgreSQL with Google Workspace integration, an offline-first field app, AI-driven analytics and a WhatsApp/SMS communications engine.

What we're building includes: — A hard-gated dispatch board with real-time staff and site availability enforcement — An offline-first field app for technicians with compliance gates, photo capture, and digital RAMS signing — A full HR, rota, and training compliance module — An automated invoicing and debt chase engine — An AI nudge engine for yield coaching and operational alerts — A computer vision module for pest identification

Who we're looking for:

A Technical Architect who has built and shipped a SaaS or field operations product before. Someone who owns architecture decisions, communicates clearly in plain English and treats deadlines as commitments not suggestions. Karachi preferred but open to remote anywhere in Pakistan.

What working with us looks like:

Weekly video calls. A shared project board you update. We are looking for someone experienced with proven track record that can actually build this and prove it.

How to apply:

Send me a DM with three things: the most complex system you've personally built, your honest assessment of the tech stack described above and your availability.

If this sounds like your kind of project — let's talk.

post.text:
🚩 HR vs. New Employee: Ek "Prem" Katha 😂

😄Jab Pehli Baar Resume Select Hota Hai

HR ka Reaction: "Mil gaya! Mere sapno ka candidate mil gaya! Iska package budget mein hai aur skills extra-ordinary hain. Bhagwan ka banda hai ye!" 🙌

Employee ka Reaction: "Wah! 50 jagah apply kiya tha, kahin se toh call aaya. Chalo ab jaldi se glassdoor par salary aur reviews check karta hoon." 🕵️‍♂️

😄Jab Salary Negotiation Hoti Hai

HR: "Dekhiye, hum aapko 'Work Culture' aur 'Learning Opportunity' de rahe hain. Paisa toh sirf ek number hai." 🧘‍♂️

Employee: "Sir, learning se ghar ka rent nahi bharta. Market standard ke hisaab se thoda 'CTC' badha dijiye  💸

😄Joining Date se Ek Din Pehle

HR ka Reaction: (Thoda Tension mein) "Call utha raha hai na? Kal pakka aayega na? Kahin ghost toh nahi kar dega? Hey Bhagwan, iska laptop ready rakhna!" 😰

Employee ka Reaction: "Kal se phir wahi 9 to 6 ki ghulami... Aaj ki raat jee bhar ke so leta hoon. Kal suit pehen ke 'Professional' bhi toh dikhna hai!" 😴

😄Joining ke Waqt

HR: "Welcome to the Family! Hum yahan sab doston ki tarah rehte hain (Jab tak target poora ho)." 🤗

Employee: "Family toh theek hai, par ye batao lunch break kab hota hai aur 'Coffee Machine' free hai na?" ☕

Moral of the Story: HR ko 'Candidate' chahiye aur Employee ko 'Offer Letter'. Dono ek dusre ko aise dekhte hain jaise "Hum Aapke Hain Koun!" lekin asliyat mein dono "Hum Saath Saath Hain" (Sirf Notice Period tak). 🏃‍♂️💨😁😄😃

author.name: Fastorial
author.headline: 25 followers

post.text:
InDrive Just Escalated Pakistan’s Delivery Wars With A Bold Bet On Quick-Commerce. 

By acquiring KraveMart, a rising grocery delivery startup, inDrive isn’t just dipping its toes into on-demand groceries; it’s doubling down on becoming the next super app for emerging markets. This move gives inDrive immediate access to crucial market real estate: dark stores and supplier networks across Karachi, Lahore, and Islamabad, the beating hearts of Pakistan's consumer economy. 

Why does this matter? Quick-commerce is a brutal game (just ask Getir and Gopuff), but inDrive’s edge lies in its operational DNA. By applying its cashflow-positive, negotiation-driven ride-hailing model to Pakistan’s hyperlocal grocery sector, it might just crack the unit economics problem where others burned billions. With delivery costs in Karachi at 80 cents, well below Western benchmarks, inDrive could leverage this advantage to scale rapidly. 

But let’s zoom out. This isn’t just about groceries; it’s about super app dominance. Can inDrive’s platform-first strategy allow it to cross-sell ride-hailing, delivery, and more while navigating Pakistan’s inflation and economic instability? And with Foodpanda holding 65% of the market and entrenched logistics, disruption won’t come easy. 

Could we be watching the next Grab or Gojek rise in South Asia—or will the cracks of quick-commerce simply widen under inDrive’s weight? 

What do you think: Is combining ride-hailing and quick-commerce under one platform genius or a risky overextension? 

Follow me for more insights into global tech plays shaping markets. 

#TechInnovation #SuperApps #QuickCommerce #EmergingMarkets #ProductStrategy

Hires also increased more than 1.5× in Sales and Artist roles, and by 40% in Fullstack and Golang. Among large categories, Design saw the biggest growth, with hires up almost 20%.

The largest decline was in DevOps, where the number of hired candidates dropped by 36%. Hiring also fell more than average in Business Analyst, Marketing, QA Automation, and Lead Generation.

Over the last three months, QA specialists were hired the most. The same was true last winter, although frontend developers led in the fall.

Besides QA and JavaScript, categories with 100+ hires over the last three months include Design, Project Management, Marketing, and Python.

post.text:
🚀 Latest Node.js Features Every Developer Should Know (2026)

Node.js continues to evolve rapidly, bringing powerful features that improve performance, security, and developer experience. Here are some of the latest updates shaping modern backend development 👇

🔥 1. Built-in Fetch API
No need for libraries like axios or node-fetch anymore. Node.js now supports the native "fetch()" API for making HTTP requests directly.

⚡ 2. Native Web APIs in Node.js
Node now includes browser-like APIs such as:
• Web Streams API
• FormData, Blob, File
• Headers, Request, Response
• Web Crypto API

This makes full-stack JavaScript development easier and reduces dependency on external packages.

🧪 3. Built-in Test Runner
Node.js now has a native test runner with watch mode and coverage support — eliminating the need for external tools like Jest for many projects.

🔒 4. Permission Model for Security
You can restrict access to the file system, environment variables, and network using permission flags like:
"node --permission-fs=read-only app.js"
This adds stronger runtime security for applications.

⚙️ 5. Performance Improvements with New V8 Engine
Latest Node versions include upgraded V8 engines with:
• Faster JSON processing
• Better memory efficiency
• Improved async performance
• Faster startup time

🌐 6. Native WebSocket & Streaming Support
Modern Node versions provide improved Web Streams and real-time capabilities for building scalable APIs and real-time applications.

📦 7. Less Dependency on npm Packages
Node.js is moving toward a “native-first” ecosystem, meaning many features previously requiring npm packages are now built into the runtime.

💡 Conclusion
Node.js is becoming more powerful with built-in tools, better security, and improved performance — making it one of the best platforms for scalable backend and full-stack applications.

#NodeJS #JavaScript #BackendDevelopment #WebDevelopment #SoftwareEngineering #Developers

You can build APIs incredibly fast.

Many major companies still rely on Node.js:

• Netflix

And when paired with React or Next.js, it becomes an incredibly productive full-stack environment.

post.text:
🚀 Why is Node.js Single-Threaded (And Still Faster)?
Most people think more threads = more speed. But in the world of high-concurrency web servers, Node.js proves that sometimes, less is more. 🧵

If you've ever wondered why a single-threaded runtime is the backbone of companies like Netflix and PayPal, here is the breakdown:

1️⃣ The Multithreading Trap 🪤
Traditional servers (like Tomcat) create a new thread for every request. Sounds great, right? Until you hit the limits:

Context Switching: The CPU spends more time "jumping" between threads than actually working.

Memory Bloat: Each thread consumes a chunk of RAM.

Race Conditions: Managing shared memory between threads is a synchronization nightmare.

2️⃣ The Node.js Secret: The Event Loop 🎡
Node.js doesn't "wait." While a traditional thread sits idle waiting for a database to respond, the Node.js Event Loop moves to the next task.

It handles requests like a fast-food clerk: They take your order, give you a buzzer, and immediately serve the next person. They don't stand still while your fries are cooking!

3️⃣ Why it Wins (The Benefits) ✅
Zero Thread Overhead: No communication lag between threads.

Scalability: It can handle thousands of concurrent connections with minimal memory.

Asynchronous Power: By using non-blocking I/O, Node.js stays "free" to accept new work.

4️⃣ The Catch: CPU Intensive Tasks ⚠️
Node.js isn't a silver bullet. Because it’s single-threaded, tasks like video encoding or heavy image processing can block the "Main Thread," freezing the whole server.

The Rule: Use Node.js for I/O heavy tasks (API, Real-time apps); use something else for heavy math.

💡 The Bottom Line:
Node.js isn't fast because it does one thing quickly; it's fast because it never stops to wait.

🔹Follow Himanshu Gupta ♾️ for more contents

post.text:
Node.js N-API: Foreign Function Interface in Node.js 

People think Node.js is only JavaScript. In reality, many of the fastest parts of the Node ecosystem are written in native languages like C or C++. Node N-API is the bridge that makes this possible.

N-API (Node API) is a stable Foreign Function Interface that allows Node.js to call native code safely. Instead of rewriting performance-critical logic in JavaScript, developers can use highly optimized native libraries and expose them directly to Node.

∆ What problem does it solve?

JavaScript is great for developer speed, but not always for raw performance. Tasks like cryptography, image processing, compression, machine learning, or heavy math can become slow in pure JS. N-API lets Node call native code where performance matters, without breaking compatibility between Node versions.

∆ Translation layer

JavaScript speaks V8. Native libraries speak C/C++. N-API acts as the interpreter between them. It converts JS objects into native structures, runs the native function, then translates the result back to JavaScript. This allows both worlds to work together smoothly.

∆ Data and stream passing

N-API supports passing buffers, typed arrays, and streams efficiently between JS and native layers. Instead of copying large datasets repeatedly, memory can be shared through ArrayBuffer or Node buffers, making high-throughput workloads possible.

∆ Libraries you probably use that rely on N-API

Many everyday Node packages depend on native bindings under the hood:

bcrypt – password hashing
sharp – high performance image processing
better-sqlite3 – fast SQLite database access
node-canvas – graphics rendering
grpc / native database drivers

Even if you never write native code yourself, your Node applications are often already powered by it.

In simple terms: N-API allows Node.js to combine JavaScript developer flexibility with native-level performance.

- @a4arpon

This is what manual visa ops actually costs your agency.

Not in theory. In real numbers.

Swipe through. Every slide is something you deal with every single day but never stopped to calculate.

43 unread WhatsApp messages. Clients, agents, partners — all asking the same thing: "kya hua mera visa?" You scroll. You search. You can't find the update. Because nobody wrote it down.

Your Excel tracker? 5 rejections sitting in red. 2 new cases with zero updates. A VLOOKUP formula holding together your entire operation. One wrong edit and three weeks of work disappears.

And here's the number nobody talks about:
40-52% — Pakistan's average visa rejection rate.

Not because agencies lack talent.
Because of documentation errors nobody caught before submission.

Every single rejection costs PKR 15,000-50,000.
Your client's money. Your reputation. Your referral chain.

Gone. Because the system was a spreadsheet and a group chat.

post.text:
Why Forward-Thinking CTOs Are Standardizing on Node.js for Modern Enterprise Platforms
In enterprise architecture discussions today, the biggest challenge is not building software it’s building systems that scale, adapt, and deliver real-time performance globally.
That is exactly why many technology leaders are turning to Node.js as the foundation of their modern application stack.
Created by Ryan Dahl, Node.js has evolved from a JavaScript runtime into a strategic backend platform powering some of the world’s most demanding digital infrastructures.
Architecture Designed for Scale
Unlike traditional server environments, Node.js operates on a non-blocking, event-driven architecture, enabling systems to handle thousands of simultaneous connections efficiently.
This architecture is particularly powerful for API ecosystems, distributed microservices, and high-traffic digital platforms.
A Unified Technology Stack
Node.js allows organizations to standardize on JavaScript across both frontend and backend, enabling faster development cycles and stronger collaboration between engineering teams.
When combined with technologies like React, Next.js, and Express.js, enterprises can build highly modular, maintainable, and scalable applications.
This reduces technical complexity while accelerating product innovation.
Enterprise Validation at Global Scale
Some of the world’s most demanding platforms rely on Node.js in production, including:
• Netflix – for high-performance streaming services
 • PayPal – to power scalable financial systems
 • LinkedIn – for fast, data-driven user experiences
These companies chose Node.js because it enables speed, scalability, and operational efficiency at global scale.
Built for Real-Time Enterprise Applications
Modern enterprises require platforms that support instant data exchange and real-time analytics.
Node.js excels in use cases such as:
• Digital banking platforms
 • IoT ecosystems
 • Healthcare platforms
 • SaaS products
 • Real-time dashboards and analytics systems
With tools like Socket.IO, systems can deliver low-latency, event-driven communication across distributed architectures.
Strategic Advantage for CTOs
For technology leaders, adopting Node.js is not just a development choice — it’s a strategic infrastructure decision.
It enables organizations to:
1.Accelerate product development
2.Reduce infrastructure overhead
 3.Improve application scalability
4.Deliver real-time digital experiences
In an era where technology velocity determines market leadership, the right backend architecture becomes a competitive advantage.
Question for CTOs & Engineering Leaders:
Are you building your next platform using event-driven architectures, or still relying on traditional monolithic systems?
Let’s discuss how modern backend technologies are reshaping enterprise software.

post.text:
Job Title: NodeJS Developer - 3 positions
Job Company: Intellect Capital Group
Location: India

Job Description:
Intellect Capital Group is immediately hiring 3 experienced NodeJS Developers to strengthen their backend development capabilities. This role is crucial for developing robust, high-performance, and scalable server-side applications and services. Candidates must possess 7-10 years of relevant professional experience, ensuring they can handle complex system architecture and deliver reliable solutions under stringent deadlines. The positions are located in the major tech hubs of Chennai and Gurugram, and local candidates are strongly encouraged to apply.

The successful hire will work closely with frontend and DevOps teams to define system requirements, design architecture, and implement high-quality code. Focus areas include optimizing application performance, ensuring security, and contributing to overall technical innovation within the team.

Job Requirements:
• 7-10 years of professional experience as a dedicated NodeJS Developer.
• Deep expertise in JavaScript/TypeScript and the Node.js runtime environment.
• Proven experience building and consuming robust RESTful APIs and microservices.
• Advanced knowledge of performance tuning and optimization for backend systems.
• Proficiency with asynchronous programming paradigms and event-driven architecture.
• Experience with SQL and NoSQL databases, including schema design and query optimization.
• Familiarity with cloud platforms and deployment strategies for high-availability applications.
• Strong debugging and advanced troubleshooting skills for production environments.
• Ability to mentor junior developers and enforce coding standards.
• Must be a local profile or willing to relocate immediately to Chennai or Gurugram.

Interested Experts Should Apply Through: https://lnkd.in/dkZctAWT

If not interested, please comment #CFBR to help this post reach more people.
